title: Sales Tax / VAT Calculator (add tax or extract from total)
shortDescription: Add sales tax to a pre-tax amount or extract the tax from a tax-inclusive total. Works for any tax rate (sales tax, VAT, GST).
description: Toggle between 'add tax' and 'extract from total' modes, enter the amount and rate, and see the breakdown into net, tax, and gross.
metaDescription: Free sales tax / VAT / GST calculator. Add tax to a pre-tax amount or extract tax from a tax-inclusive total. Works with any rate, in your browser.
empty: Enter an amount to compute the tax breakdown.

sections.heading: Add tax vs extract tax
paragraphs: Adding tax: you have the price before tax and want the final price. Multiply by (1 + rate/100). Example: $100 at 8% sales tax = $108.

Extracting tax: you have the total a customer paid (or saw on the receipt) and need to reverse-engineer how much was tax. Divide the total by (1 + rate/100) to get the pre-tax amount; the difference is the tax. Example: $108 at 8% extracts to $100 + $8 tax. Don't multiply $108 by 8% — that gives $8.64, which is wrong.

sections.heading: Sales tax, VAT, and GST
paragraphs: Sales tax (US): added at the point of sale, varies by state and city. New York City is 8.875%, Oregon has none, California ranges 7.25-10.75% depending on city. Many US prices are advertised pre-tax and tax is added at checkout.
VAT (EU, UK): typically already included in the displayed price. UK standard rate is 20%, France is 20%, Germany is 19%. Use 'extract from total' to find the tax in a quoted price.

GST (Australia, NZ, Canada, India): a single national rate (Australia and NZ at 10% and 15% respectively, Canada has GST plus provincial taxes). India uses tiered GST rates (5/12/18/28%) by product category.

sections.heading: Common pitfalls
paragraphs: Don't multiply tax-inclusive totals by the rate. To extract tax from $108 at 8%, divide by 1.08 first. Multiplying by 8% gives the wrong answer because the $108 already includes the 8% on the original $100 — and you'd be taxing the tax.
Some products are exempt or zero-rated (groceries in many US states; books in the EU at reduced rate; etc.). When advertising prices including/excluding tax, follow your jurisdiction's rules.

howTo.name: Pick the mode
howTo.text: Add tax to a pre-tax amount, or extract tax from a tax-inclusive total.

howTo.name: Enter the amount
howTo.text: Pre-tax in 'add' mode; total in 'extract' mode.

howTo.name: Enter the rate
howTo.text: Tax rate as a percent (e.g., 8.875 for NYC, 10 for Japan, 20 for UK VAT).

howTo.name: Read the breakdown
howTo.text: Net (pre-tax), tax amount, and total are all displayed.

faq.q: Why is extract-from-total ÷ (1 + rate) instead of × rate?
faq.a: Because the total already includes the tax on the original. Multiplying gives the tax on the inclusive total, which is too much. Dividing by (1 + rate/100) reverses the addition correctly.

faq.q: What rate should I use for the US?
faq.a: US sales tax varies by state and city. Common: NYC 8.875%, LA 9.5%, Chicago 10.25%, Texas state 6.25%, Florida state 6%. Add city/county on top in many places.
Mostly yes — 10% is standard. Food and beverages other than alcohol/dining are at the reduced 8% rate.

faq.q: Are taxes the same as the displayed price?
faq.a: Depends on country. EU/UK/Japan typically advertise tax-inclusive prices. The US, Australia, and Canada often show pre-tax. Use the 'extract' mode for inclusive prices, 'add' mode for pre-tax.

faq.q: Can this handle multiple tax rates?
faq.a: Not at once. For combined rates (e.g., state + local), add them up first and use the combined rate.
Same math: 'extract from total' to see VAT and pre-VAT net. Most accounting software does this automatically.
Currency is displayed in JPY for Japanese pages, USD elsewhere. Math is currency-independent — use any unit consistently.
No. All math runs locally in your browser.
